questions.question: A fair coin is flipped. You win $3 on heads and lose $1 on tails. What is the expected profit per flip?

explanations.B: Adding both payoffs as if both happen, ($3+$1)/2, double-counts mutually exclusive outcomes.
explanations.C: This is the negative of the true EV; you swapped which outcome is the gain.
explanations.D: This counts only the heads payoff, $3/2, and ignores the tails loss.

questions.question: Two cards are drawn from a standard 52-card deck without replacement. What is the probability that both are hearts?

explanations.A: Correct. Without replacement, P = (13/52)·(12/51) = 1/17.
explanations.B: This treats the draws as independent, (1/4)², but the first item is not replaced.
explanations.C: This reduces the favorable count to 12 but forgets the pool shrinks from 52 to 51.
explanations.D: This is the probability for a single draw, not both.

questions.question: Independent trials each succeed with probability 1/2. What is the expected number of trials until the first success?

explanations.A: Correct. For a geometric distribution with success probability p = 1/2, the mean is 1/p = 2.
explanations.B: This is the per-trial success probability, not the expected wait.
explanations.C: Off by one — the geometric mean is exactly 1/p = 2, counting the successful trial.
explanations.D: 4 is on the order of the variance (≈1/p²), not the mean.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 3 times. What is the probability of getting at least one head?

explanations.A: This is P(no heads) = (1/2)^3, the complement of the event asked.
explanations.B: This is P(exactly one head) = C(3,1)/2^3; 'at least one' also includes 2, 3, … heads.
explanations.C: Correct. P(at least one head) = 1 − (1/2)^3 = 7/8.
explanations.D: 1/2 is the chance for a single flip, ignoring all 3 flips.

questions.question: A disease is present in 1% of a population. A screening test is 99% sensitive and 99% specific. A randomly chosen person tests positive. What is the probability they actually have the disease?

explanations.A: This is the test's sensitivity P(+|disease); it ignores the base rate (base-rate fallacy).
explanations.B: This is the prior prevalence, before updating on the positive result.
explanations.D: This is P(positive test) overall, the denominator of Bayes' rule, not the posterior.

questions.question: A disease is present in 1% of a population. A screening test is 90% sensitive and 90% specific. A randomly chosen person tests positive. What is the probability they actually have the disease?

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum equals 3?

explanations.A: Correct. 2 of 36 equally likely ordered pairs sum to 3, so 1/18.
explanations.B: Treating all 11 possible sums as equally likely gives 1/11, but middle sums are more common.
explanations.C: 1/36 is one specific ordered pair; the sum 3 can occur 2 ways.
explanations.D: 1/6 is the chance one die shows a given value, unrelated to the sum.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum equals 4?

explanations.A: Treating all 11 possible sums as equally likely gives 1/11, but middle sums are more common.
explanations.B: Correct. 3 of 36 equally likely ordered pairs sum to 4, so 1/12.
explanations.C: 1/36 is one specific ordered pair; the sum 4 can occur 3 ways.
explanations.D: This is P(sum = 3) = 1/18, a different sum.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum equals 5?

explanations.C: Correct. 4 of 36 equally likely ordered pairs sum to 5, so 1/9.
explanations.D: This is P(sum = 4) = 1/12, a different sum.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum equals 6?

explanations.A: Treating all 11 possible sums as equally likely gives 1/11, but middle sums are more common.
explanations.B: 1/36 is one specific ordered pair; the sum 6 can occur 5 ways.
explanations.C: This is P(sum = 5) = 1/9, a different sum.
explanations.D: Correct. 5 of 36 equally likely ordered pairs sum to 6, so 5/36.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum equals 9?

explanations.C: This is P(sum = 9) = 1/9, a different sum.
explanations.D: Correct. 3 of 36 equally likely ordered pairs sum to 10, so 1/12.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability the sum equals 11?

explanations.A: Correct. 2 of 36 equally likely ordered pairs sum to 11, so 1/18.
explanations.B: Treating all 11 possible sums as equally likely gives 1/11, but middle sums are more common.
explanations.C: 1/36 is one specific ordered pair; the sum 11 can occur 2 ways.
explanations.D: This is P(sum = 10) = 1/12, a different sum.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the expected value of the larger of the two numbers (the maximum)?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the mean of a single die; the maximum of two is larger.
explanations.C: A round guess; the exact value is 161/36 ≈ 4.47.
explanations.D: 91/36 ≈ 2.53 is E[min], not E[max].

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the expected value of the smaller of the two numbers (the minimum)?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the mean of a single die; the minimum of two is smaller.
explanations.B: That is E[max], not E[min].
explanations.D: A round guess; the exact value is 91/36 ≈ 2.53.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability that the maximum of the two numbers is 6?

explanations.A: 1/6 is P(a single die shows 6); with two dice a 6 is more likely.
explanations.B: 1/36 is P(both dice are 6), not P(max=6).
explanations.C: Too high — there are 11 favorable ordered pairs out of 36.
explanations.D: Correct. P(max=6) = P(at least one 6) = 1 − (5/6)² = 11/36.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability that the minimum of the two numbers is 1?

explanations.A: Correct. P(min=1) = P(at least one 1) = 1 − (5/6)² = 11/36.
explanations.B: 1/6 is P(a single die shows 1).
explanations.C: 1/36 is P(both dice are 1), not P(min=1).
explanations.D: Too high — 11/36 is the exact value.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the probability that both dice are at most 4?

explanations.A: 2/3 is P(one die ≤ 4); both dice must satisfy it.
explanations.C: This ignores that both dice must be ≤ 4.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. What is the expected absolute difference between the two numbers?

explanations.A: The signed difference averages 0, but the absolute value is positive.
explanations.B: Off by a factor of 2 — the total of |i−j| is 70, giving 70/36.
explanations.C: Correct. E[|i−j|] = (Σ over all 36 pairs of |i−j|)/36 = 70/36 = 35/18 ≈ 1.94.
explanations.D: 7/2 is E[sum], not E[|difference|].

questions.question: A Bernoulli random variable equals 1 with probability 1/3 and 0 otherwise. What is its variance?

explanations.A: This is the mean p, not the variance.
explanations.B: This is p², but the variance is p(1−p).
explanations.C: This is 1−p (the probability of 0), not the variance.

questions.question: A fair six-sided die is rolled. What is the variance of the outcome?

explanations.B: 3.5 is the mean E[X], not the variance.
explanations.C: 91/6 is E[X²]; the variance subtracts (E[X])².
explanations.D: 6 is the number of faces, not the variance.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 10 times. What is the variance of the number of heads?

explanations.A: 5 = np is the mean number of heads, not the variance.
explanations.C: 10 is the number of flips n, not the variance.
explanations.D: This uses np(1+p) = 10·(1/2)·(3/2); variance is np(1−p).

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 100 times. What is the variance of the number of heads?

explanations.A: 50 = np is the mean, not the variance.
explanations.B: 100 is n, not the variance.
explanations.D: This uses np(1+p); the variance is np(1−p) = 25.

questions.question: You are long a large inventory of a contract and want to reduce risk. How should you skew your two-sided quote?
options: Raise both bid and ask to buy even more
Widen the spread symmetrically and keep the midpoint fixed
Only raise the ask and keep the bid unchanged
Lower both bid and ask to encourage selling out of the position

explanations.A: Raising quotes invites more buying, increasing an already-long position.
explanations.B: Widening manages risk but does not actively reduce an existing long position.
explanations.C: Raising the ask makes selling harder — the opposite of reducing a long position.
explanations.D: Correct. Skewing quotes down makes you more likely to sell and less likely to buy, trimming a long position.

questions.question: Informed traders consistently buy from you just before the price rises. You are suffering from:

explanations.A: Correct. Trading against better-informed counterparties who pick the right side is adverse selection.
explanations.B: Inventory risk is price drift on held positions, not being systematically picked off by informed flow.
explanations.C: Slippage is execution cost when exiting, not losing to informed counterparties.
explanations.D: Carry is a holding return; this scenario describes a loss to informed traders.

questions.question: Fair value is 50. You quote 48 bid / 52 ask and a counterparty lifts your ask at 52. Was the trade good for you?
options: Yes — you sold at 52, two above fair value, capturing edge
Only if the price later rises

explanations.A: Selling above fair value is profitable in expectation for a market maker.
explanations.B: Correct. Selling at 52 versus a fair value of 50 books +2 of expected edge.
explanations.C: Fills away from fair value are not break-even; price versus fair determines edge.
explanations.D: Edge is measured against fair value at the time of the trade, not future moves.

questions.question: Why do market makers quote a spread instead of trading at a single price?
options: To make the math harder for clients
To be compensated for inventory risk and adverse selection

explanations.A: Spreads are an economic choice, not a regulatory mandate.
explanations.B: Spreads exist for risk compensation, not to confuse clients.
explanations.C: Correct. The spread pays for holding risk and for trading against possibly-informed flow.
explanations.D: The spread is precisely the maker's compensation for risk.

questions.question: Your estimate of fair value just became much more uncertain. The right response is to:
options: Tighten your spread to win more trades

explanations.A: Tightening when uncertain invites being picked off on both sides.
explanations.B: Quotes should reflect updated uncertainty.
explanations.C: Pulling out forfeits all flow; usually you widen rather than vanish.
explanations.D: Correct. More uncertainty means more adverse-selection risk, so quotes widen.

questions.question: Edge captured on a single fill is best described as:
options: The distance between your fill price and the true fair value
The full spread regardless of side
Zero unless the price later moves

explanations.A: Correct. Edge per fill is |fill price − fair value|.
explanations.B: Half-spread is the edge only when fair value sits at the midpoint.
explanations.C: A single fill captures at most the half-spread, not the full spread.
explanations.D: Edge is booked relative to fair value immediately, independent of later moves.

questions.question: You quote 99 bid / 101 ask around a fair value of 100, and trades arrive equally on both sides. Per fill, you expect to:
options: Earn the full spread of 2 per fill
Earn roughly the half-spread (about 1) per fill on average

explanations.A: The full spread is earned over a round trip (a buy and a sell), not per single fill.
explanations.B: Correct. Each fill is about 1 away from the fair value of 100, so the average edge is the half-spread.
explanations.C: Fills land away from fair value, so each carries expected edge.
explanations.D: Random two-sided flow around fair value is profitable for the maker, not loss-making.

questions.question: A counterparty only trades with you when they have superior information. On average this leaves you with:
options: Losing positions, because you are adversely selected

explanations.A: Facing only informed flow produces losses, not gains.
explanations.B: Pure informed flow is negative for the maker, not neutral.
explanations.C: Correct. Trading only against better information means you systematically take the worse side.
explanations.D: You still acquire inventory; it is simply adversely selected.

questions.question: You are highly confident about fair value and competing hard for flow. You should:
options: Widen the spread to be safe
Quote a tighter spread to win more trades

explanations.A: Widening when confident needlessly cedes flow to competitors.
explanations.B: High confidence is a reason to quote actively, not withdraw.
explanations.C: Quotes should track your fair-value estimate, not be random.
explanations.D: Correct. Lower uncertainty lets you compete with tighter quotes while still capturing edge.

questions.question: Fair value is 20 and your bid is 18. A trader sells to you at 18. Relative to fair value, this fill is:
options: Profitable — you bought 2 below fair value
A loss — buying always costs money
Impossible to judge without the ask

explanations.A: Correct. Buying at 18 against a fair value of 20 books +2 of edge.
explanations.B: Buying below fair value is profitable in expectation.
explanations.C: Price versus fair value, not the act of trading, determines edge.
explanations.D: The bid price versus fair value already determines the edge on this buy.

questions.question: 5 people each toss their hat into a pile, then each picks a hat uniformly at random. What is the expected number of people who get their own hat back?

explanations.A: This is the probability one specific person gets their hat, not the expected total.
explanations.B: Correct. By linearity, E = Σ P(person i gets own hat) = 5·(1/5) = 1.
explanations.C: Not everyone gets their hat; by linearity the expectation is exactly 1.
explanations.D: On average one person does get their hat back — the expectation is 1, not 0.

questions.question: 50 people each toss their hat into a pile, then each picks a hat uniformly at random. What is the expected number of people who get their own hat back?

explanations.A: This is the probability one specific person gets their hat, not the expected total.
explanations.B: Not everyone gets their hat; by linearity the expectation is exactly 1.
explanations.C: Correct. By linearity, E = Σ P(person i gets own hat) = 50·(1/50) = 1.
explanations.D: On average one person does get their hat back — the expectation is 1, not 0.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled 12 times. What is the expected number of sixes?

explanations.A: That is the expected sixes for a single roll, not 12.
explanations.B: 12 is the number of rolls, not the expected count of sixes.
explanations.C: This uses 1/36 per roll; each roll yields a six with probability 1/6.
explanations.D: Correct. E = 12·(1/6) = 2 by linearity of expectation.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled 10 times. What is the expected number of sixes?

explanations.B: That is the expected sixes for a single roll.
explanations.C: 10 is the number of rolls, not the expected count.
explanations.D: This uses 1/36 per roll instead of 1/6.

questions.question: Five cards are dealt from a standard 52-card deck. What is the expected number of aces in the hand?

explanations.A: That is the expected number of aces in a single card.
explanations.B: Correct. By linearity, E = 5·(4/52) = 20/52 = 5/13.
explanations.C: This uses 1 ace instead of the 4 aces per drawn card.
explanations.D: This confuses the four suits with the four aces.

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 7 times. What is the expected number of heads?

explanations.A: That would require every flip to be heads.
explanations.B: That is the expectation for a single flip.
explanations.D: A rounded guess; the exact expectation is 3.5.

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.3, what is P(not A)?

explanations.A: This is P(A) itself, not its complement.
explanations.B: 1 would mean 'not A' is certain, impossible when P(A) > 0.
explanations.C: 0 would mean 'not A' is impossible, but A is not certain.
explanations.D: Correct. P(not A) = 1 − P(A) = 1 − 0.3 = 0.7.

questions.question: If P(A) = 0.25, what is P(not A)?

explanations.A: Correct. P(not A) = 1 − P(A) = 1 − 0.25 = 0.75.

questions.question: Events A and B are mutually exclusive with P(A) = 0.2 and P(B) = 0.3. What is P(A or B)?

explanations.A: 0.06 = P(A)·P(B) is for the AND of independent events, not OR.
explanations.B: Correct. For mutually exclusive events, P(A or B) = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5.
explanations.C: This subtracts the probabilities; for OR of exclusive events you add them.
explanations.D: This ignores event B entirely.

questions.question: Independent events A and B have P(A) = 0.5 and P(B) = 0.4. What is P(A and B)?

explanations.A: 0.9 = P(A) + P(B), which is for OR (and ignores overlap).
explanations.B: This ignores event B.
explanations.C: Correct. For independent events, P(A and B) = 0.5 × 0.4 = 0.2.
explanations.D: This averages the two probabilities instead of multiplying.

questions.question: In how many distinct orders can you arrange 3 distinct books on a shelf?

explanations.A: (3−1)! = 2 fixes one item in place; all 3 can move.
explanations.B: 3² is not the number of orderings.
explanations.C: 2^3 counts subsets, not orderings.
explanations.D: Correct. 3 distinct items arrange in 3! = 6 ways.

questions.question: In how many distinct orders can you arrange 4 distinct books on a shelf?

explanations.A: Correct. 4 distinct items arrange in 4! = 24 ways.
explanations.B: (4−1)! = 6 fixes one item in place; all 4 can move.
explanations.C: 4² is not the number of orderings.
explanations.D: Doubling 4 is not how arrangements grow.

questions.question: In how many distinct orders can you arrange 5 people in a row?

explanations.A: (5−1)! = 24 fixes one item in place; all 5 can move.
explanations.B: Correct. 5 distinct items arrange in 5! = 120 ways.
explanations.C: 5² is not the number of orderings.
explanations.D: Doubling 5 is not how arrangements grow.

questions.question: How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in the word "BOOK"?

explanations.A: This ignores the repeated O; divide by 2!.
explanations.B: This over-divides; only the O repeats (÷2!), giving 12.
explanations.C: This is not how arrangements are counted.
explanations.D: Correct. "BOOK" has repeated letters, so the count is 4! divided by the factorials of the repeats = 12.

questions.question: How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in the word "LEVEL"?

explanations.A: Correct. "LEVEL" has repeated letters, so the count is 5! divided by the factorials of the repeats = 30.
explanations.B: This ignores repeats; divide by 2!·2! for L and E.
explanations.C: This divides by only one 2!; both L and E repeat (÷2!·2!).
explanations.D: This over-divides; the answer is 30.

questions.question: How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in the word "NOON"?

explanations.A: This ignores repeats; ÷(2!·2!) for N and O.
explanations.B: Correct. "NOON" has repeated letters, so the count is 4! divided by the factorials of the repeats = 6.
explanations.C: This divides by only one 2!; both letters repeat.
explanations.D: This over-divides; the answer is 6.

questions.question: How many distinct arrangements are there of the letters in the word "STATS"?

explanations.B: This divides by only one 2!.
explanations.C: Correct. "STATS" has repeated letters, so the count is 5! divided by the factorials of the repeats = 30.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. Given that at least one die shows a 1, what is the probability that both dice show 1?

explanations.A: This treats the other die as independent (1/6), ignoring the conditioning.
explanations.B: 1/36 is the unconditional P(both 1), before learning at least one is 1.
explanations.C: This miscounts the conditioning set as 12 ordered pairs instead of 11.
explanations.D: Correct. There are 11 ordered pairs with at least one 1; only (1,1) has both, so 1/11.

questions.question: A game pays $10 with probability 1/4 and $0 otherwise. What is the expected payout?

explanations.A: This is the prize, not the probability-weighted average.
explanations.C: This uses probability 1/2; the win probability is 1/4.
explanations.D: Slight miscalculation; (1/4)·10 = 2.5.

questions.question: A bet wins $100 with probability 1/10; otherwise you lose $10. What is the expected profit?

explanations.A: This counts only the (1/10)·100 win and ignores the likely $10 loss.
explanations.B: This subtracts 100 − 10 as if both happen exactly once.
explanations.D: This counts only the loss and ignores the winning case.

questions.question: Three envelopes contain $1, $2, and $3; you pick one at random. What is the expected amount?

explanations.A: This is the total of all envelopes, not the average.
explanations.B: This is the largest amount, not the expectation.
explanations.C: This averages only two of the three values.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled and you are paid in dollars equal to the number shown. What is the expected payout?

explanations.B: This rounds down; the exact mean is 3.5.
explanations.C: This is the maximum face, not the average.
explanations.D: This is the sum of the faces, not the average.

questions.question: A wheel pays $5 with probability 0.2 and $1 with probability 0.8. What is the expected payout?

explanations.A: This averages 5 and 1 without weighting by probability.
explanations.C: This is the top prize, not the average.
explanations.D: This ignores the 0.2 chance of the $5 payout.

questions.question: A lottery ticket pays $1000 with probability 1/1000 and $0 otherwise. What is the expected payout?

explanations.A: This is the jackpot, not its probability-weighted value.
explanations.B: The expectation is positive, $1, even though winning is rare.
explanations.D: This miscalculates 1000/1000 as 10.

questions.question: A bet wins $3 with probability 1/3 and loses $1 with probability 2/3. What is the expected profit?

explanations.A: This treats the loss as 2/3·$1.50 to cancel; the true loss leg is $2/3, leaving +$1/3.
explanations.B: This counts only the (1/3)·$3 win and ignores the loss.
explanations.C: Sign error; the win outweighs the loss, giving +$1/3.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled: an even number pays $2, an odd number loses $1. What is the expected profit?

explanations.B: Sign error; the even payout exceeds the odd loss, so EV is +$0.50.
explanations.C: This counts only the $2 even-payout and ignores the odd-number loss.
explanations.D: This treats the payoffs as equal in size, but $2 ≠ $1.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a heart?

explanations.A: 1/13 is the chance of a specific rank, not a suit.
explanations.B: Correct. 13 hearts of 52 cards = 13/52 = 1/4.
explanations.C: 1/52 is the chance of one specific card.
explanations.D: This inverts the ratio; hearts are 13/52 = 1/4.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is an ace?

explanations.A: 1/4 is the chance of a given suit, not a rank.
explanations.B: 1/52 is one specific card.
explanations.C: Correct. 4 aces of 52 = 4/52 = 1/13.
explanations.D: This forgets to divide by the 4 suits; 4/52 = 1/13.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a face card (J, Q, or K)?

explanations.A: This counts only one of the three face ranks.
explanations.B: This forgets there are 4 of each face rank; 12/52 = 3/13.
explanations.C: This confuses 13 cards of a suit with the 12 face cards.
explanations.D: Correct. 12 face cards of 52 = 12/52 = 3/13.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a red king?

explanations.A: Correct. 2 red kings of 52 = 2/52 = 1/26.
explanations.B: This counts one specific king, but there are two red kings.
explanations.C: This is P(any king), counting all 4 kings.
explanations.D: This inverts the ratio; 2/52 = 1/26.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a spade or a queen?

explanations.A: This adds 13 + 4 without subtracting the queen of spades counted twice.
explanations.B: Correct. 13 spades + 4 queens − 1 (queen of spades) = 16 cards, 16/52 = 4/13.
explanations.C: This counts only the spades and ignores the off-suit queens.
explanations.D: This counts only the four queens.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a number card from 2 to 10?

explanations.A: This mistakenly includes an extra rank among the 9 number ranks.
explanations.B: This forgets the 4 suits; 36/52 = 9/13.
explanations.C: Correct. Ranks 2–10 give 9 ranks × 4 suits = 36 cards, 36/52 = 9/13.
explanations.D: A rough guess; the exact value is 9/13.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a black ace?

explanations.A: This counts one ace, but there are two black aces.
explanations.B: This counts all four aces, not just the black ones.
explanations.C: This inverts the ratio.
explanations.D: Correct. 2 black aces (spades, clubs) of 52 = 2/52 = 1/26.

questions.question: One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability it is a J, Q, K, or A?

explanations.A: Correct. 16 such cards (J, Q, K, A) of 52 = 16/52 = 4/13.
explanations.B: This omits the aces, counting only J, Q, K.
explanations.C: This counts only one of the four high ranks.
explanations.D: This confuses the four high ranks with the four suits.

questions.question: What is the smallest number of people that guarantees at least two share the same birth month?

explanations.A: With 12 people each could have a different month; you need one more.
explanations.B: Correct. With 12 months, 13 people force a repeat by the pigeonhole principle.
explanations.C: Two people might fall in different months; a guarantee needs 13.
explanations.D: More than needed — 13 already forces a shared month.

questions.question: Ignoring leap years (365 days), what is the smallest number of people that guarantees two share a birthday?

explanations.A: 365 people could all have distinct birthdays; you need one more.
explanations.B: Roughly half is far short of a guarantee.
explanations.C: Correct. With 365 possible birthdays, 366 people force a match (pigeonhole).
explanations.D: 23 gives about a 50% chance, not a guarantee.
